engagement.name: NorthBridge Compliance Solutions - Simulated FDD Deck

slides.objective: Establish engagement context, target company, and period coverage for the simulated FDD deck.
evidence: Target company: NorthBridge Compliance Solutions, Inc. (NorthBridge); HQ: Toronto, Ontario; founded 2011.
Engagement type: simulated buyside Financial Due Diligence (FDD) deck for generator testing.
Financial periods presented: FY2023, FY2024, FY2025, and TTM ended Jun-2026.
Currency convention: USD millions unless otherwise noted.

slides.objective: Provide the reader a structured navigation of the deck sections and how evidence flows to conclusions.
evidence: Section 1: Executive summary and transaction-relevant conclusions (slides s03–s05).
Section 2: Business overview and revenue quality (slides s06–s11).
Section 3: P&L walkthrough (slides s12–s18).
Section 4: Balance sheet, working capital profile, and cash conversion (slides s19–s24).
Section 5: QoE bridge and adjustment support status (slides s25–s32).
Section 6: Net working capital and peg implications (slides s33–s38).
Section 7: Key diligence risks and reporting environment (slides s39–s41).
Section 8: Appendices and exhibit placeholders (slides s42–s45).

slides.objective: Summarize the headline diligence conclusions, including earnings durability, normalization themes, and decision-level risks.
evidence: Revenue grew from USD 125.0m (FY23) to USD 160.0m (FY25) and USD 168.0m (TTM Jun-26), driven primarily by SaaS subscription expansion.
Recurring SaaS revenue represented ~82.1% of TTM Jun-26 revenue (management reporting; preliminary).
Reported EBITDA was USD 29.0m in TTM Jun-26 (17.3% margin) with cash conversion of ~77% (CFO USD 22.4m).
Preliminary QoE bridge indicates normalized EBITDA of USD 32.4m (net adjustments +USD 3.4m); several high-impact items remain support-pending.
Operating NWC is structurally negative (reported USD -28.0m at Jun-26) driven by deferred revenue; peg mechanics should avoid unintended purchase price outcomes.

slides.objective: Highlight the reported financial trajectory, margin profile, and cash conversion in a transaction framing.
evidence: Reported gross margin remained stable at ~64% across FY23–TTM Jun-26 (gross profit USD 108.0m on USD 168.0m revenue in TTM).
EBITDA increased from USD 22.0m (FY23) to USD 29.0m (TTM Jun-26), while EBITDA margin remained ~17–18% as opex scaled with growth.
Net debt reduced from USD 42.0m (FY23) to USD 29.0m (TTM Jun-26) due to sustained free cash flow and term debt paydown.
TTM Jun-26 free cash flow before distributions was ~USD 14.9m (CFO USD 22.4m less capex/capitalized software USD 7.5m).
Transaction implication: leverage capacity appears supported at current run-rate, but valuation should reference normalized EBITDA subject to support tie-out.

slides.objective: Summarize preliminary transaction considerations: normalized earnings base, working capital peg, and priority diligence close items.
evidence: Normalized EBITDA (preliminary) of USD 32.4m implies normalized EBITDA margin of ~19.3% on TTM Jun-26 revenue.
Largest support-pending QoE themes: milestone revenue cut-off (services), capitalization policy normalization, and resolution of legal settlement support.
Reported NWC at Jun-26 is USD -28.0m; adjusted NWC after identified normalizations is USD -26.5m (preliminary).
Peg recommendation: consider a negative peg range (e.g., -26m to -27m) based on trailing averages and deal definition alignment (subject to monthly schedule tie-out).
Next-step focus: finalize support for top 5 QoE and top 5 NWC adjustments; confirm SPA definitions (debt-like vs working capital) to avoid double-counting.

slides.objective: Describe the operating model, how the company makes money, and what features of revenue quality matter for transaction risk.
evidence: NorthBridge provides cloud-based EHSQ compliance software and implementation services to regulated industrial customers across North America.
Primary revenue streams: (i) SaaS subscriptions billed annually (recurring), (ii) professional services for implementation and configuration (project-based).
Scale: TTM Jun-26 revenue USD 168.0m; customer base ~420 customers / ~1,450 managed sites (management reporting; preliminary).
Footprint: HQ Toronto with delivery hubs in Denver, Dallas, and Atlanta; blended onshore/offshore delivery model for implementation and support.
Diligence context: revenue durability depends on renewal retention, usage expansion, and cut-off discipline for milestone-based services revenue.
Transaction implication: earnings quality is sensitive to service delivery timing and capitalization policies; buyer protections should focus on cut-off and policy consistency.

slides.objective: Quantify revenue mix (recurring vs non-recurring) and interpret implications for earnings durability and valuation.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
Revenue grew from USD 125.0m (FY23) to USD 168.0m (TTM Jun-26).
SaaS subscription revenue increased from USD 98.0m (FY23) to USD 138.0m (TTM Jun-26), representing ~82.1% of total revenue (management reporting; preliminary).
Professional services revenue remained ~USD 29.0m–USD 30.0m per period (FY24–TTM), indicating delivery capacity constraints rather than demand constraints.
Revenue quality implication: higher recurring mix supports valuation durability, but services timing/cut-off can introduce quarter-to-quarter EBITDA volatility.
Follow-up required: validate subscription vs services mapping from billing system to GL and reconcile ARR reporting to GAAP revenue.

slides.objective: Explain the drivers of topline growth and isolate structural vs timing elements relevant to sustainability.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
Total revenue increased by USD 43.0m from FY23 (USD 125.0m) to TTM Jun-26 (USD 168.0m).
Management attribution (preliminary): growth driven by net new ARR (~USD 16m in FY25), annual price uplift (~3–4%), and cross-sell expansion within existing accounts.
Services revenue is relatively flat, consistent with implementation capacity constraints; backlog at Jun-26 estimated at ~USD 18m (management reporting; support pending).
Churn and contraction are concentrated in smaller manufacturing customers; retention in utilities segment is reported higher (support pending by cohort).
Transaction implication: base-case revenue durability is supported by subscription mix, but downside sensitivity should incorporate (i) pipeline conversion risk and (ii) services cut-off variability.

slides.objective: Assess customer concentration, retention, and related downside sensitivity for the transaction.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
Customer concentration (FY25): top 10 customers ~22% of revenue; largest customer ~4.8% (management reporting; support pending to billing extract).
Retention (FY25): gross retention ~93%; net revenue retention ~109% driven by module expansion and regulatory-driven add-ons (management reporting; support pending).
Average subscription contract term ~2.3 years with annual billing; renewal calendar is weighted to Q4 due to legacy contract start dates.
Risk implication: concentration is moderate but still relevant for downside cases; largest 3 customers collectively drive ~12% of revenue and should be stressed in valuation sensitivity.
Follow-up required: reconcile concentration metrics to invoicing data and confirm no undisclosed side letters impacting pricing or renewal terms.

slides.objective: Summarize bookings, backlog, and implementation pipeline to support forward-looking revenue and margin considerations.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Bookings trend (management reporting; preliminary): FY24 bookings ~USD 34m; FY25 bookings ~USD 38m with conversion to ARR weighted to H1 following contract signature.
Services backlog at Jun-26 estimated at ~USD 18m (~60% of next 12 months services revenue), indicating near-term revenue visibility but also delivery capacity dependence.
Implementation pipeline: backlog aging has increased (average implementation cycle ~4.2 months in FY25 vs ~3.5 months in FY23), contributing to contractor usage.

Transaction implication: sustained growth requires scaling implementation capacity without margin dilution; diligence should focus on utilization, subcontractor reliance, and backlog burn-down assumptions.
Data request: monthly bookings/backlog rollforward with definitions and reconciliation to billings and GAAP revenue.

slides.objective: Document revenue recognition mechanics and billing terms, highlighting cut-off and contract accounting risks.
evidence: SaaS subscriptions are typically billed annually in advance; this drives deferred revenue balances (current deferred revenue {fmt_m(38.0)} at Jun-26).
Professional services are primarily milestone-based with revenue recognized upon delivery; contract assets (unbilled receivables) were {fmt_m(9.0)} at Jun-26.
Reported operating NWC is negative (Jun-26 NWC {fmt_m(nwc['TTM Jun-2026'])}), reflecting advance billings and deferred revenue rather than working capital strain.
Cut-off sensitivity: services milestone acceleration can overstate both revenue and EBITDA in the measurement period; a preliminary QoE adjustment of -USD 0.6m has been identified (support pending).
Transaction implication: closing accounts and peg should explicitly define treatment of deferred revenue and contract assets to avoid double-counting between QoE and NWC adjustments.

slides.objective: Present reported P&L trend as the baseline for QoE normalization and valuation analysis.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Reported revenue increased from USD 125.0m (FY23) to USD 160.0m (FY25) and USD 168.0m (TTM Jun-26).
Gross profit increased from USD 80.0m (FY23) to USD 108.0m (TTM Jun-26); gross margin remained ~64%.
Reported EBITDA increased from USD 22.0m (FY23) to USD 29.0m (TTM Jun-26); margin ~17.3% in TTM.
Depreciation & amortization increased to USD 9.0m in TTM, consistent with capitalized software growth and acquired intangibles amortization.
Normalization watch items: services cut-off, capitalization policy consistency, and non-recurring operating costs embedded in opex.

slides.objective: Explain gross margin stability and isolate the drivers (mix, hosting efficiency, services utilization) that affect sustainable EBITDA.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
Blended gross margin remained stable at ~64% from FY23 through TTM Jun-26 (FY25: 64.4%; TTM: 64.3%).

Subscription gross margin is estimated at ~78% (hosting and customer success costs), while services gross margin is estimated at ~33% (labor utilization-driven) (management reporting; support pending).
Hosting costs increased with customer count, but unit economics improved modestly due to cloud-commitment discounts (support pending to invoices).
Services margin is sensitive to subcontractor use and project delays; contractor usage increased in FY25 to address backlog (linked to QoE adjustment QA07).
Transaction implication: downside sensitivity should assume either flat subscription margin with services variability, or a partial unwind of cloud discounts at renewal.

slides.objective: Decompose operating expenses to explain EBITDA margin trajectory and identify normalization considerations.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Total opex (ex D&A) increased from USD 58.0m (FY23) to USD 79.0m (TTM Jun-26) as the company invested in sales capacity and product development.
Sales & Marketing increased from USD 28.0m (FY23) to USD 39.0m (TTM), remaining ~22–23% of revenue.
R&D increased from USD 17.0m (FY23) to USD 22.0m (TTM) and is supplemented by capitalized development cash outflows of USD 4.5m (TTM).
G&A increased from USD 13.0m (FY23) to USD 18.0m (TTM), including discrete non-recurring legal and ERP-related spend (see QoE register).
Transaction implication: EBITDA margin stability reflects reinvestment; normalized EBITDA is more sensitive to non-recurring opex items than to structural margin compression.

slides.objective: Assess sales efficiency and scalability of growth (CAC payback, pipeline conversion) and identify risks to forward run-rate.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
S&M spend remained ~22–23% of revenue, while revenue growth moderated from +13.6% (FY24) to +12.7% (FY25) and +5.0% (TTM vs FY25).
Management KPIs (preliminary): CAC payback ~20–24 months; net new ARR in FY25 ~USD 16m; pipeline conversion ~22% from qualified to closed-won (support pending).
Sales capacity expanded in FY24–FY25 (headcount additions), driving near-term opex pressure ahead of ARR realization.
Transaction implication: base-case assumes continued ARR expansion; downside case should incorporate lower conversion or elongated sales cycles without immediate opex step-down.
Diligence ask: tie CRM bookings/pipeline to invoicing and confirm definition consistency for CAC and payback metrics.

slides.objective: Evaluate R&D investment and capitalization practices to support product roadmap and assess normalization risk.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Expensed R&D was USD 22.0m in TTM Jun-26; capitalized software development cash outflows were USD 4.5m in the same period.
Implied development capitalization rate is ~17% of total development spend in TTM (cap dev 4.5 / (R&D 22.0 + cap dev 4.5)).

Management indicates capitalization increased in FY25 due to a major platform re-architecture; QoE adjustment QA12 assumes a normalized capitalization rate closer to historical policy baseline (support pending).

Product roadmap implication: sustaining ARR expansion requires continued investment; buyer should validate whether roadmap spend is appropriately classified (expense vs capitalize) and consistent with GAAP policy.
Data request: capitalization policy memo, project time tracking extracts, and rollforward from capitalized dev to amortization schedule.

slides.objective: Explain G&A trend and separate structural cost base from non-recurring items relevant to normalized earnings.
evidence: G&A increased from USD 13.0m (FY23) to USD 18.0m (TTM Jun-26) as the company scaled finance, security, and compliance functions.
Non-recurring components are embedded in G&A, including ERP integration costs (QA01), transaction advisory fees (QA04), and a discrete legal settlement (QA03).
Reporting process maturity is improving but remains reliant on manual journals during ERP transition; this increases the risk of cut-off and accrual variability (see reporting environment section).

Transaction implication: normalized earnings should exclude non-recurring G&A, but the buyer should assume a higher steady-state corporate cost base post-close (public-company readiness, controls uplift).
Follow-up required: reconcile G&A department allocations and validate that one-time items are properly segregated in the GL.

slides.objective: Bridge EBITDA movement from FY23 to TTM Jun-26 to identify the primary operational drivers of profitability.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
Reported EBITDA increased by USD 7.0m from FY23 (USD 22.0m) to TTM Jun-26 (USD 29.0m).
Gross profit increased by USD 28.0m over the same period (FY23 USD 80.0m to TTM USD 108.0m), driven by revenue growth at stable gross margin.
Opex (ex D&A) increased by USD 21.0m (FY23 USD 58.0m to TTM USD 79.0m), reflecting investment in sales capacity and product development.
Key interpretation: EBITDA growth is volume-led, but margin stability indicates incremental gross profit is being reinvested rather than fully dropping to EBITDA.
Transaction implication: normalized EBITDA should focus on removing discrete, non-recurring costs rather than assuming structural margin expansion.

slides.objective: Provide a stratified balance sheet view to identify working capital drivers, debt-like items, and capital intensity.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Total assets were USD 163.0m at Jun-26, including cash USD 13.0m, AR USD 28.0m, contract assets USD 9.0m, and goodwill/intangibles USD 77.0m.
Total liabilities were USD 121.0m at Jun-26, including current deferred revenue USD 38.0m, term debt USD 38.0m, and non-current deferred revenue USD 8.0m.
Deferred revenue is the primary driver of negative operating working capital and cash conversion; diligence should validate deferred revenue rollforward and service obligations.
Debt-like watch items: litigation reserve and ERP payable (see working capital adjustments WC02 and WC10) and any off-balance sheet commitments.
Transaction implication: balance sheet valuation should focus on true debt-like items versus operating deferred revenue to avoid over-adjusting purchase price.

slides.objective: Explain the operating working capital profile and isolate the components that drive variability and peg risk.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Operating NWC moved from USD -21.0m (FY23) to USD -28.0m (Jun-26), primarily due to deferred revenue growth (current deferred revenue USD 28.0m to USD 38.0m).
AR increased from USD 22.0m (FY23) to USD 28.0m (Jun-26) in line with revenue; contract assets increased from USD 6.0m to USD 9.0m reflecting milestone-based services activity.
Operating implication: structurally negative NWC supports cash conversion, but increases sensitivity to revenue cut-off and contract asset collectability.
Peg implication: small classification/cut-off errors in deferred revenue and contract assets can drive meaningful purchase price adjustments given negative peg baseline.
Follow-up required: monthly working capital schedule and reconciliation to AR subledger, billing system, and deferred revenue rollforward.

slides.objective: Summarize net debt, leverage, and covenant headroom to support valuation and financing discussions.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
Net debt declined from USD 42.0m (FY23) to USD 29.0m (Jun-26) as term debt amortized and cash stabilized.
Leverage at Jun-26 is ~1.0x net debt / reported EBITDA and ~0.9x net debt / normalized EBITDA (preliminary).
Debt terms (management-provided; support pending): senior term loan with maturity in 2029; variable rate (SOFR + ~3.25%).
Transaction implication: financing capacity appears available, but buyer should stress normalized EBITDA downside case and ensure debt-like items are captured consistently with SPA.
Data request: debt agreement, covenant definitions, and quarterly covenant calculations through Jun-26.

slides.objective: Bridge EBITDA to cash conversion and identify key cash drivers relevant to valuation and debt sizing.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
TTM Jun-26 CFO was USD 22.4m on reported EBITDA of USD 29.0m (~77% cash conversion).
TTM investing cash outflows were USD 10.5m, including PP&E capex USD 3.0m and capitalized software development USD 4.5m.
Free cash flow before financing/distributions was ~USD 14.9m (CFO USD 22.4m less capex/cap dev USD 7.5m).
Cash flow sensitivity: deferred revenue growth is a meaningful cash tailwind; if growth slows, cash conversion may revert toward 65–75% absent opex step-down.
Transaction implication: debt sizing should consider a cash conversion range and capex/cap dev requirements to sustain ARR growth.

slides.objective: Explain capital intensity and intangible asset profile, including any impairment or capitalization risks relevant to valuation.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Capitalized software (net) increased from USD 14.0m (FY23) to USD 19.0m (Jun-26), reflecting ongoing platform investment.
Other intangibles (net) declined from USD 25.0m (FY23) to USD 17.0m (Jun-26) due to amortization (D&A USD 9.0m in TTM).
Goodwill remained USD 60.0m across periods; no impairment testing performed as part of this simulated package.
Transaction implication: capitalization practices influence both EBITDA (expense vs capitalize) and future amortization; buyer should validate policy compliance and project eligibility.
Follow-up required: fixed asset register, intangible rollforward, and capitalization eligibility testing for major platform projects.

slides.objective: Identify debt-like items and balance sheet topics that influence enterprise value to equity value bridge and SPA definitions.
evidence: Term debt was USD 38.0m at Jun-26; lease liabilities were USD 4.0m. Net debt was USD 29.0m.
Deferred revenue (current + non-current) totaled USD 46.0m at Jun-26 and is a core operating liability; treatment should align with peg/closing accounts to avoid double-counting.
Potential debt-like items identified for definition alignment: litigation reserve (WC10), ERP vendor payable (WC02), and change-in-control bonuses (QA06) if paid pre-close.
Transaction implication: EV-to-equity bridge should clearly separate operating liabilities (deferred revenue) from debt-like items to prevent price disputes.
Data request: detailed schedule of accruals within accrued expenses and other current liabilities to support debt-like vs NWC classification.

slides.objective: Bridge reported EBITDA to normalized EBITDA and summarize the main adjustment themes with support status.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
TTM Jun-26 reported EBITDA: USD 29.0m (17.3% margin).
Identified adjustments (net): +USD 3.4m across 13 items; preliminary normalized EBITDA: USD 32.4m (19.3% margin).
Largest add-backs: ERP integration costs (QA01 +1.2), restructuring/severance (QA02 +0.8), transaction advisory fees (QA04 +0.7).
Largest deductions: services revenue cut-off (QA11 -0.6), excess capitalization of development costs (QA12 -0.4), insurance proceeds removal (QA10 -0.3).
Support status: ~4 adjustments supported, ~4 pending, remainder partial; valuation should be based on normalized range until pending items are resolved.

slides.objective: Provide a complete register of identified QoE adjustments, including recurring flag, support status, and transaction relevance.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
13 adjustments identified for TTM Jun-26; 9 add-backs totaling +USD 4.9m and 4 deductions totaling -USD 1.5m (net +USD 3.4m).
Recurring adjustments (policy/run-rate): related-party hosting markup (QA09) and capitalization/fee accrual normalizations (QA12, QA13).
Support mix: supported (QA01, QA04, QA06, QA08, QA10); partial (QA02, QA05, QA07, QA13); pending (QA03, QA09, QA11, QA12).
Transaction implication: recurring adjustments should be reflected in run-rate forecasts and integration plans; non-recurring items inform one-time cost expectations but not valuation base.
Follow-up required: tie-out each adjustment to GL detail, invoices/contracts, and (where applicable) payroll registers and customer delivery evidence.

slides.objective: Deep-dive the ERP integration adjustment: quantify costs, confirm one-time nature, and describe residual risks.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
ERP integration and systems costs adjustment (QA01): +USD 1.2m in TTM Jun-26; management attributes costs to external consultants, data migration, and parallel-run support.
Timeline: primary go-live completed in Q1 FY26; parallel-run costs expected to decline materially in H2 FY26 (support pending to project plan).
Residual risk: post-close stabilization and optimization spend may continue, particularly around reporting automation and controls uplift.
Transaction implication: treat QA01 as non-recurring for valuation, but include a one-time integration budget in the post-close plan; confirm no capitalization misclassification.
Support request: vendor SOWs, invoices, and project cost center GL extracts reconciled to QA01 amount.

slides.objective: Deep-dive restructuring and retention adjustments: quantify costs and validate that savings are not already embedded in run-rate.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Restructuring/severance (QA02): +USD 0.8m; change-in-control retention bonuses (QA06): +USD 0.3m in TTM Jun-26.
Headcount actions: customer success consolidation and role rationalization; management indicates run-rate savings of ~USD 0.6m annualized (support pending).
Risk of double-counting: confirm savings are not already reflected in the TTM base period and that contractor usage is not offsetting permanent reductions.
Transaction implication: buyer should consider a run-rate cost base that includes ongoing capacity required to deliver backlog; validate savings versus service quality impacts.
Support request: HR termination register, payroll extracts, bonus plan documents, and timing of headcount reductions.

slides.objective: Deep-dive legal and insurance items to ensure sustainable EBITDA excludes discrete matters and does not embed one-time gains/losses.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Legal settlement and outside counsel (QA03): +USD 0.5m identified; support pending to settlement agreement and invoices; management asserts non-recurring.
Insurance proceeds (QA10): -USD 0.3m removed from EBITDA as a non-recurring gain; supported by accounting entry classification (support provided).
Residual risk: confirm no additional contingent liabilities or customer disputes that would imply recurring legal spend above baseline.
Transaction implication: unresolved legal matters can create both EBITDA noise and potential debt-like exposures; SPA should address indemnities and specific reserves.
Data request: legal claims summary, settlement documentation, and insurance correspondence supporting QA03/QA10.

slides.objective: Deep-dive timing and policy normalizations (cut-off and capitalization) that can materially affect both EBITDA and working capital.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Services revenue cut-off (QA11): -USD 0.6m pending confirmation of delivery status for milestone-based implementations; impacts both revenue and contract asset / deferred revenue classification.
Excess capitalization of development costs (QA12): -USD 0.4m to normalize capitalization rate; support pending to policy memo and time tracking.
Under-accrued SaaS platform fees (QA13): -USD 0.2m to align run-rate vendor expense with contract schedule (partial support).
Transaction implication: these items are high priority because they can shift both normalized EBITDA and NWC peg; diligence should ensure consistent treatment to avoid double-counting.
Follow-up required: contract-level delivery evidence, capitalization eligibility testing, and vendor contract schedules tied to accruals.

slides.objective: Present sensitivity of normalized EBITDA to key open items and define the diligence close plan for support-pending adjustments.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
Pending QoE items (QA03, QA09, QA11, QA12) represent ~USD 1.7m of gross adjustment magnitude; net impact is ~USD -0.3m.
Base-case normalized EBITDA: USD 32.4m; illustrative downside case (if QA11/QA12 confirmed but QA03/QA09 not supported) approximates ~USD 31.7m (assumption).

Sensitivity drivers: (i) milestone delivery evidence for QA11, (ii) capitalization policy compliance for QA12, (iii) legal settlement documentation for QA03, (iv) related-party contract terms for QA09.
Transaction implication: valuation and debt sizing should use a range until support is obtained; buyer should align purchase agreement language to unresolved QoE and debt-like items.
Close plan: prioritize support for items comprising >USD 0.5m and document resolution path (support obtained vs reclassified vs removed).

slides.objective: Conclude the QoE analysis with decision-level implications for valuation, financing, and SPA negotiation priorities.
evidence: Preliminary normalized EBITDA of USD 32.4m (TTM Jun-26) represents a +USD 3.4m uplift versus reported EBITDA and is driven primarily by non-recurring items.
Two adjustments (services cut-off and capitalization policy) drive most of the downside risk and require detailed testing to avoid valuation overstatement.
At Jun-26 net debt of USD 29.0m, leverage is ~0.9x on normalized EBITDA; debt capacity appears supported but should incorporate a downside EBITDA case.
SPA implication: define treatment of transaction costs, legal reserves, and deferred revenue consistently between QoE, debt-like, and working capital true-up to prevent double-counting.
Recommendation: treat normalized EBITDA as a range pending support tie-out; include targeted diligence deliverables as conditions to finalizing price mechanics.

slides.objective: Summarize NWC profile and propose a preliminary peg view aligned to deal mechanics.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
Reported operating NWC was USD -28.0m at Jun-26 versus USD -21.0m at FY23, reflecting deferred revenue growth and subscription billing terms.
After identified normalizations (10 items; net +USD 1.5m), adjusted NWC is USD -26.5m (preliminary).
Preliminary peg implication: negative peg is appropriate given business model; propose -26m to -27m range subject to monthly schedule and SPA definition alignment.
Key drivers of variability: deferred revenue renewal seasonality (Q4 heavy), services milestone billings (contract assets), and timing of bonus and vendor accruals.
Transaction implication: purchase price true-up sensitivity is high due to negative peg; narrow definitions and robust closing accounts procedures reduce dispute risk.

slides.objective: Assess seasonality and measurement-period considerations to calibrate the peg and mitigate closing date arbitrage.
evidence: [CHART PLACEHOLDER]
Management indicates NWC becomes more negative in Q4 due to annual renewals and upfront billings; monthly NWC is estimated to range from ~-24m to ~-30m (support pending to monthly schedule).
If closing occurs in Q4, peg should reference a seasonally-adjusted average rather than a point-in-time balance to prevent transfer of value via renewal timing.
Services milestone billing can create short-term contract asset spikes; peg methodology should specify treatment of unapproved change orders and disputed balances.
Transaction implication: consider a trailing 12-month average peg or a seasonality-adjusted peg by quarter; define measurement period in SPA to avoid opportunistic timing.
Data request: monthly balance sheet (last 24 months) with reconciliations for AR, contract assets, deferred revenue, and accrual components.

slides.objective: Review AR and contract assets (unbilled receivables) for collectability and cut-off risks that impact peg and closing adjustment.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
AR was USD 28.0m at Jun-26 with implied DSO ~60 days (management reporting; support pending to subledger).
Aged/disputed AR reserve adjustment (WC04): -USD 0.6m proposed pending validation of dispute status and subsequent collections.
Contract assets were USD 9.0m at Jun-26; adjustment WC05 proposes -USD 0.4m to exclude unapproved change orders from peg.
Transaction implication: AR and contract assets are primary sources of peg volatility; buyer should require detailed aging, dispute logs, and proof of delivery for milestone billings.
Follow-up required: AR aging, credit memos/credits log, contract asset schedule by project, and post-period cash receipts testing.

slides.objective: Review deferred revenue and contract liabilities for completeness and delivery alignment, given its outsized impact on negative NWC.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Deferred revenue totaled USD 46.0m at Jun-26 (current USD 38.0m; non-current USD 8.0m), reflecting annual upfront subscription billings.
Deferred revenue cut-off true-up (WC09): +USD 0.3m increase to peg based on services delivered but not released from deferral (support pending).
Risk: misalignment between delivery status and deferral can impact both EBITDA (QoE cut-off) and peg; definitions must avoid double-counting the same timing issue.
Transaction implication: buyer should validate deferred revenue rollforward by product and term, and assess cost-to-serve to confirm deferred revenue is not economically debt-like.
Data request: deferred revenue rollforward, billing system extracts, and delivery evidence for services components.

slides.objective: Summarize identified working capital adjustments and translate them into a preliminary adjusted peg recommendation.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
10 working capital adjustments identified for Jun-26; net impact +USD 1.5m to reported NWC (i.e., less negative).
High peg-relevance items: deal-related accruals (+1.1), litigation reserve (+0.8), ERP payable (+0.7), and AR reserve (-0.6) (support pending/partial).
Adjusted NWC estimate: USD -26.5m versus reported USD -28.0m.
Transaction implication: peg should be set on adjusted NWC with explicit exclusions for transaction-specific and debt-like items; otherwise the true-up could systematically transfer value.
Follow-up required: map each adjustment to SPA definitions and confirm which items are expected to be settled pre-close versus left in the business.

slides.objective: Translate working capital findings into closing mechanics recommendations and quantify potential purchase price adjustment sensitivity.
evidence: Peg mechanics sensitivity: a USD 1.0m swing in NWC at close translates 1:1 to purchase price adjustment under standard true-up provisions.

Given negative peg baseline, classification decisions (deferred revenue vs debt-like, transaction accruals vs operating accruals) can create disproportionate dispute risk if definitions are not precise.

Recommended mechanics: (i) define NWC inclusions/exclusions clearly (taxes, transaction accruals, leases), (ii) specify measurement date and methodology (average vs point-in-time), (iii) require detailed schedules for high-volatility accounts.
Interaction with QoE: timing and policy items (services cut-off, capitalization policy) should be resolved in one place (either QoE or NWC) with explicit cross-references.
Next-step ask: align legal/SPA teams on proposed NWC definition and produce a pro forma closing statement template prior to signing.

slides.objective: Consolidate the primary diligence risks, quantify where possible, and propose mitigations or deal protections.
evidence: Revenue cut-off risk on milestone-based services: preliminary QoE adjustment -USD 0.6m (QA11) and related NWC adjustment (WC05/WC09) indicate timing sensitivity.
Capitalization policy risk: higher capitalization rate drives EBITDA uplift; QA12 (-USD 0.4m) remains support-pending and requires policy and time-tracking validation.
Customer concentration/retention risk: top 10 customers ~22% of FY25 revenue; NRR/GRR metrics are management-reported and require tie-out to billings.
Data security and remediation risk: QA05 (+USD 0.4m) indicates a discrete incident; buyer should confirm remediation completeness and any ongoing obligations.
Mitigations: targeted purchase agreement definitions, specific indemnities for discrete matters, and diligence completion conditions for high-impact open items.

slides.objective: Assess financial reporting process maturity and highlight areas that affect diligence confidence and post-close integration effort.
evidence: Month-end close is reported at ~8 business days; ERP transition has increased manual workarounds and reconciliations (management walkthrough; support pending).
Manual journals represent ~18% of quarter-end entries, concentrated in revenue cut-off, capitalization, and accrual true-ups (support pending to JE listing).
Key process gaps (preliminary): inconsistent project accounting for services milestones, limited automated deferred revenue rollforward, and reliance on spreadsheets for capitalization eligibility.
Transaction implication: higher reporting risk increases the probability of working capital and QoE disputes; buyer should plan for early controls uplift and reporting automation post-close.
Recommended remediation: strengthen cut-off controls, implement contract accounting subledger reporting, and formalize capitalization governance with periodic review.

slides.objective: List the unresolved diligence items required to finalize QoE, peg, and EV-to-equity bridge conclusions.
evidence: Priority open items (support pending): QA03 (legal settlement), QA09 (related-party hosting markup), QA11 (services cut-off), QA12 (capitalization normalization), WC02 (ERP payable classification), WC04/WC05 (AR/contract asset reserves), WC10 (litigation reserve).

Data requests to close: AR aging + subsequent receipts; deferred revenue rollforward; services delivery evidence for milestone billings; capitalization policy memo + time tracking; JE listing for quarter-end.
Definition alignment: confirm SPA treatment for deferred revenue, taxes payable, transaction accruals, and any debt-like reserves to avoid double-counting across QoE and NWC.
Timeline assumption: package is prepared on a simulated basis; in a live deal, these items would be prioritized in the final 2–3 weeks before signing/close.
Transaction implication: absent support closure, valuation should reference a normalized EBITDA and NWC peg range rather than a point estimate.

slides.objective: Provide ARR cohort / retention support for the main-deck revenue quality conclusions.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Management-reported ARR increased from ~USD 118m (FY23) to ~USD 148m (FY25) and ~USD 155m (TTM Jun-26) (support pending to billing export).
Net revenue retention is reported at ~108–109% in FY25–TTM, implying expansion offsets churn; cohort analysis required to validate by segment.
Transaction implication: revenue durability and valuation multiple depend on retention quality; reconcile ARR to GAAP revenue and validate cohort-level retention drivers.
Data request: ARR bridge by customer cohort, churn log, and price uplift schedule tied to contract terms.

slides.objective: Support operating expense analysis with payroll/headcount trends and preserve the analyst-owned exhibit placeholder.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Exhibit handoff: replace placeholder with payroll bridge tied to HRIS and payroll registers (by function and location).
Total cash compensation (management reporting; preliminary): ~USD 44m (FY23) to ~USD 52m (FY25) and ~USD 54m (TTM Jun-26).
Headcount (preliminary): ~560 FTE (FY23) to ~610 FTE (FY25) with hiring concentrated in sales and product; contractor usage increased in FY25 for implementation backlog.
Potential normalization linkage: severance/retention (QA02/QA06) and contractor backlog costs (QA07) should reconcile to payroll and vendor invoices.
Follow-up required: FTE roster, comp mix (base/bonus/commission), and reconciliation of payroll to GL by department.

slides.objective: Provide capex and capitalized software detail to support cash conversion and capitalization policy conclusions.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
TTM Jun-26 PP&E capex was USD 3.0m and capitalized software development was USD 4.5m (cash flow bridge).
Major spend themes (management reporting; support pending): platform re-architecture, security tooling, and customer success automation.

Transaction implication: validate that capitalized software additions meet eligibility criteria and that amortization profile is consistent with useful lives; this directly impacts EBITDA normalization (QA12).
Data request: capex ledger, capitalized project listing, and rollforward from additions to amortization.

slides.objective: Provide debt and lease schedule detail supporting net debt and covenant commentary.
evidence: [TABLE IMAGE PLACEHOLDER]
Term debt balance was USD 38.0m at Jun-26; lease liabilities were USD 4.0m (balance sheet).
Illustrative terms (management-provided; support pending): variable-rate term loan (SOFR + ~3.25%), maturity 2029, quarterly amortization with optional prepayment.
Covenant framework (support pending): net leverage covenant <3.5x and minimum liquidity covenant; confirm definitions and add-backs permitted.
Transaction implication: debt-like item definitions and covenant headroom should be validated early to avoid closing delays and post-close surprises.
Data request: executed credit agreement, amortization schedule, and historical covenant compliance calculations.
